sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 9

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 9:1
items.html:
Spiritual motion means a renewal of disparity of form.
It still remains to clarify the matter of time and motion, which we encounter in nearly every word of this wisdom. Know that spiritual motion is not like tangible motion from place to place; rather, the intention is upon a renewal of form, for we call every renewal of form by the name "motion." That renewal — the disparity of form newly made in a spiritual entity, differing from the general form that preceded it in that spiritual entity — is discerned as having divided off and grown distant from that spiritual entity, coming out with a name and a governance of its own. In this it is entirely like a corporeal essence from which some part is separated, which moves off and goes from place to place. Therefore the renewal of form is called by the name "motion."

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 9:2
items.html:
Spiritual time means a certain number of renewals of disparities of form, arising from one another. "Before" and "after" mean cause and consequence.
And the matter of time, in its spiritual definition, you will understand thus: the entire concept of time with us is nothing but a sensation of motions. The imaginative faculty in a person depicts and weaves a certain number of motions, which it sensed one after another, and renders them in the imagination as a certain "time" — such that if a person and his surroundings were in a state of absolute rest, he would know nothing at all of the concept of time. So it is in spiritual matters: a certain sum of renewals of form, discerned as spiritual motions, as above, entangled in one another by way of cause and consequence, is what we call "time" in spirituality. And the matter of "before" and "after" always means cause and consequence.
